course_description: This course is an introductory level examination of the basic principles of taxation law in Australia, with particular emphasis on the topics required by professional accounting bodies for accreditation purposes.

requirements.restrictions: Not for students enrolled in LLB(Hons)(#64), LLB(Hons)(#48), LLB, LLB (Grad Entry), LLB(Hons) duals and LLB dual programs
requirements.assumed_background: The taxation of business income depends on the legal structure/s used to conduct the business. The taxation of passive income, for example dividends and rental income, depends on the ownership rights of the parties that own the assets that give rise to the passive income. The most common legal structures associated with these activities are sole traders, partnerships, companies and discretionary trusts. Students are expected to be familiar with the ownership rights associated with sole traders, partnerships and companies from their studies of LAWS1100 Business Law (or its equivalent).

timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Additional Timetable Information
Lectures

Lectures are timetabled and conducted in person on campus. A recording of each lecture will be made available in Learning Resources on the course Blackboard site. Students are not required to preference a lecture in My Timetable. Students are expected to attend or watch the weekly lectures in the week when each lecture takes place as the success of the Tutorial Program relies on students' knowledge gained from the previous weeks’ lectures.

Tutorials

Students are required to preference for a tutorial group via My Timetable (available through my.UQ dashboard) so that they choose a tutorial group that fits in with their personal circumstance. Students are required to enrol in a tutorial group and to attend their tutorials in the tutorial group they are enrolled in according to their personal timetable . Attendance at tutorials conducted in Teaching Weeks 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 12 and 13 are compulsory and is a pre-requisite to earn assessment marks. It is not possible to attend another tutorial group, as summative tutorial assessment is linked to the tutorial group each student is enrolled in on their own personal timetable. It is therefore very important that each student proactively preferences a tutorial group that suits their personal circumstances. Note: LAWS3101 Teaching staff do not have access to the timetabling system and are therefore unable to move a student’s sign-on from one tutorial class to another.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims:

The aim of LAWS3101 is to equip you with the skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and apply the sources of taxation law and the regulatory framework in which taxation law is administered in Australia, to solve real-world income tax problems.
Analyse events and transactions to construct and explain the calculation of taxable income and taxes payable.
Analyse the taxation issues associated with non-complex international transactions in an income tax and Goods and Service Tax context.
Apply Goods and Services Tax to the treatment of business transactions.
Analyse employment entitlements to apply income tax and Fringe Benefits Tax to remuneration.

assessment_summary.title: Tutorials
assessment_summary.due_date: See the Task Description for due dates and times.

assessment_details.title: Tutorials
assessment_details.due_date: See the Task Description for due dates and times.
assessment_details.task_description:
Tutorials provide students with guided opportunities to practice their skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings in-class. There are 10 tutorials across the semester.

For flexibility, summative assessment marks are available in 8 of the tutorials. The maximum marks per tutorial is 5%. The maximum mark for all tutorials is 30%, calculated on a cumulative (aggregate) basis for all summative tutorial assessment opportunities. It is a pre-requisite to attend your own timetabled scheduled tutorial for the full duration of the tutorial to be eligible to earn summative assessment marks, irrespective of the mode of tutorial assessment task submission. It is not possible to attend another tutorial group. Therefore, attendance at your own timetabled scheduled tutorial according to your own personal timetable is compulsory for tutorials conducted in Teaching Weeks: 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 12 and 13 to earn summative assessment marks. It is your responsibility to sign the attendance register each week as evidence of your attendance.

Summative tutorial assessments are individual assessment activities. Students must work alone. The use of generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools is not permitted. Any attempted use of AI may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Due dates and times for tutorials conducted in Teaching Weeks 3, 5 and 6

Conducted as in-class open book quizzes. Students must have access to a computer and internet access during their tutorial class. Students will commence their online quizzes in their own timetabled scheduled tutorial according to their personal timetable, and only when their tutors instruct them to do so during a specific short time window period. Therefore, all students enrolled in a particular tutorial group will commence and complete their online quiz at the same time. It is not possible to complete the online quiz at a different time unless prior approval have been granted through a formal application for extension. Each quiz closes automatically at the end of the test duration. Therefore, late submission is not possible. Students have one attempt at completing the quiz.

Due dates and times (AEST) for tutorials conducted in Teaching Weeks 4, 7, 9, 12, 13

Each tutorial involves the submission of typed up answers in one Word file. There is no particular font or font size that students must use, and no word count limit. Students must type their name, surname and student ID in a page header.

The due dates and times for each tutorial submission is as follows:

Teaching Week 4 tutorial: 5 pm on Thursday 7 March 2024.

Teaching Week 7 tutorial: 10 am on Monday 8 April 2024.

Teaching Week 9 tutorial: 5 pm on Thursday 18 April 2024.

Teaching Week 12 tutorial: 5 pm on Thursday 9 May 2024.

Teaching Week 13 tutorial: 5 pm on Thursday 16 May 2024.

Students must retain proof of their submission through Turnitin, and are responsible for using the correct Turnitin link to submit each tutorial. Students are responsible for ensuring that their tutorial answers are submitted successfully through Turnitin. Email submission is not permitted and is not an acceptable form of submission.

Late submission penalties apply as set out in section 5.3 of this course profile.

Tutorials conducted in Teaching Weeks 4, 7, 9, 12 and 13: Typed up answers in a single Word file submitted online through the relevant Turnitin link on the course Blackboard site. Submission due dates and times set out in the Task Description.

assessment_details.title: In-Semester Examination (timed, non-invigilated)
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The In-Semester Examination is a timed, non-invigilated, online exam, completed in the course Blackboard site.

This is an individual assessment activity. Students must work alone.

The use of generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools is not permitted. Any attempted use of AI may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Specified materials permitted: Any written, printed, or electronic materials stored locally or in the cloud (e.g. textbooks, notes, summaries, dictionaries), and materials on the course Blackboard site. Materials may be annotated. Students are not permitted to access internet materials during this examination, other than the course Blackboard site and an online version of the prescribed textbook.

The In-Semester Examination covers Topics 1 to 5.

Duration: 50 minutes plus 10 minutes planning time.

As the In-Semester Examination is a timed exam, students are expected to know all of the work covered in the exam and the In-Semester Examination is set on that basis. Therefore, no time allowance is made for students to look up answers.

All students must commence the exam at 10:15 am (Brisbane time zone) on Tuesday 16 April 2024.

All students must complete and submit their exam by 11:15 am (Brisbane time zone) on Tuesday 16 April 2024, regardless of their starting time. Late submissions will incur a penalty in accordance with UQ policy.

assessment_details.title: End-of-semester Examination
assessment_details.format: Short answer, Short essay, Extended essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The Final Exam is an on-campus, in-person, invigilated exam.

The Final Exam covers Topics 3 to 11.

Any written or printed materials (e.g. textbook, notes pages, dictionaries) is permitted in the exam. Materials may be annotated.

Students must use a UQ-approved calculator.

Duration: 120 minutes plus 10 minutes planning time.

As the Final Exam is a timed exam, students are expected to know all of the work covered in the exam and the Final Exam is set on that basis. Therefore, no time allowance is made for students to look up answers.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of Generative AI may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

learning_resources.required_resources:
Principles of Taxation Law 2024 Edition . Thomson Reuters. K Sadiq (General Editor). Paper copy ISBN 9780455248110. E-book ISBN 9780455248127.

​Permitted materials in the Final Exam are hard copy (paper) materials only. If you purchase the E-book, you must print the pages you wish to use during the Final Exam.

Due to the rules-based nature of LAWS3101, significant independent study from Principles of Taxation Law 2024 is required. It is essential to have continuous access to this textbook. Students should have their own copy of Principles of Taxation Law 2024. For some tutorials, students will be required to work directly from Principles of Taxation Law 2024.

Due to the number of changes to tax laws in 2023, the 2023 edition of Principles of Taxation Law is outdated.

Topic Learning guides. Individual Topic Learning guides will be released weekly on the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ (Blackboard). Students will find these in the teaching week folders in Learning Resources. Topic Learning guides do not replace the prescribed textbook and do not include the course material covered in the course .
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
The following acts will be referenced during the course:

Income Tax Assessment Act 1997

Income tax Assessment Act 1936

A New Tax System (GST) Act 1999

Fringe Benefits Tax Assessment Act 1986.

These are available online:

Australian Legal Information Institute, www.austlii.edu.au

ATO, www.ato.gov.au

The relevant parts of the aforementioned legislation will be included in the learning guides for the relevant topics if students are required to study these. Students will not be required to search these online resources to prepare for assessments.

Students may find resources such as the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) website, incorporating the ATO Legal Database helpful.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS3101 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition) .

For a short summary of the complete Australian Guide to Legal Citation , see the UQ Library's Referencing Guide to the AGLC .

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Learn. UQ (Blackboard) Website

Course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the AskUs Library Help – askus@library.uq.edu.au .

The University of Queensland Library offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Which resources will help you to meet the assessment criteria in LAWS3101?

Taxation law in Australia is rules-based. To meet the assessment criteria in LAWS3101, your answers must include references to specific sections and divisions, specific case law and specific ATO Rulings that are listed in the Topic Learning guides. It is essential to consult each topic’s Learning guide to know:

Which specific material from Principles of Taxation Law 2024 you are required to study for each topic.

Which specific sections and divisions from statute law and which ATO Rulings numbers to include in your answers in assessments to meet the assessment criteria.

Which specific case law citations to include in your answers in assessments to meet the assessment criteria.

Where can you find more questions and marking guides for answers to tax problems?

Each Topic Learning guide includes a series of questions with criteria and marking guides to help you to assess your own understanding of course materials.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

LAWS3101 Teaching staff have weekly consultation hours that are open to all students. See the details of consultation hours on the course Blackboard site. Students are warmly encouraged to attend, to discuss technical questions and their learning approach to LAWS3101.

Tutorial classes provide students with a guided opportunity each week to practice their skills to solve real-world tax problems and to receive feedback. To maximise the benefits of tutorials, students are expected to prepare for and attend all of their own timetabled scheduled tutorial classes, and to take the opportunity to ask questions.

Each Topic Learning guide includes a series of questions and answer marking guides to help students evaluate whether they meet the specific assessment criteria and performance standards in LAWS3101.

Written feedback are provided in relation to tutorial exercises. However, as students are expected to attend their tutorials, comprehensive answers to tutorials are not released.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the School’s policy ( https://law.uq.edu.au/student-support/information-students/assessment-and-examinations ).

Deferred Examinations ( in-semester and end-of-semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with the Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred in-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of in-semester examinations. Deferred end-of-semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to my.UQ for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

course_introduction:
LAWS3101 is an introductory course in taxation law that covers income taxes levied on the taxable income of a range of taxpayers, Goods and Service Tax (GST) and Fringe Benefits Tax (FBT). LAWS3101 will set you on a path so that you are able to think about the real-world implications of taxation. LAWS3101 will equip you with the skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO (Australian Taxation Office) Rulings.

What makes LAWS3101 different to your other courses?

Taxation law is complex, even at an introductory level , and taxation i s a con tinually changing area of the law. Your biggest challenge in LAWS3101 is to navigate the rules-based nature of taxation law which is unlike the principles-based nature of accounting and finance courses. Each LAWS3101 topic covers specific sections and divisions of statute law, specific case law and ATO Rulings that are essential elements of your answers in assessments in order to meet the assessment criteria and performance standards in the course. You should adapt your learning approach accordingly.

Accreditation

LAWS3101 is accredited by Chartered Accountants Australia New Zealand (CAANZ) and CPA Australia. The scope and depth of topics covered, and assessment in the course, aligns to accreditation.

Janine McMaster-Kirkwood Prize in Income Tax Law

The student with the best performance in their first attempt at LAWS3101 will be recommended for this prize. One prize will be awarded. This prize is not shared between students.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The Tutorial Program was adapted at the beginning of 2023 so that students are able to develop their skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings in-class during tutorials. To achieve this goal, students must prepare for their tutorial classes by following the steps set out in each tutorial document. Students are required to attend their own timetabled scheduled tutorial class to be eligible for tutorial assessment marks. For this reason, it is of the utmost importance that students preference a tutorial group that fits their personal circumstances.
other_learning_activities_information:
VERY IMPORTANT - Please read.

Your weekly time-investment in LAWS3101

Taxation law is complex, even at an introductory level. To achieve your learning goals, you must be prepared to make a serious 8-hour time commitment to your studies of LAWS3101 each week.

Due to accreditation, the volume and depth of work covered in LAWS3101 is significantly more than in most of your other courses. To navigate this volume and complexity, you must expect to devote more time to LAWS3101 than you might be accustomed to in other courses. You must use the Topic Learning guides to navigate the coursework.

Taxation law in Australia is rules-based. This means that you must know, apply and include the following in your answers in assessments to meet the assessment criteria: specific section numbers, specific division numbers, specific case law and specific ATO Rulings numbers as stipulated in the Topic Learning guides, to demonstrate that you know how to solve real-world tax problems at the performance standards set for the course. This level of detail is very different to the principles-based nature of accounting and finance courses. Plan to adapt your usual learning approach to make the best use of your 8-hour time investment in LAWS3101 each week.

Planning your weekly learning activities

To help you plan your learning activities, we provide you with a daily diary in the weekly folders in Learning Resources on Blackboard that also directs you to a range of valuable course resources. Your most important learning activity each week is to study the course topic materials from Principles of Taxation Law 2024 (PoTL). To know which materials to study, you must consult the Topic Learning guides in the weekly folders in Learning Resources on the course Blackboard site. Plan to study the topic materials from PoTL. It is not an effective study technique to read these materials . Be prepared to undertake significant self-directed learning from PoTL each week.

Lectures

Lectures are timetabled and conducted in person. You should attend the weekly lectures because the lecture content is assumed knowledge for, and forms the basis of the next week’s tutorial exercises. If you are unable to attend the in-person lecture, you should watch the recording that will be released to Blackboard in that week, to avoid falling behind.

During lectures we introduce you to each topic and discuss more complex aspects of each topic in preparation for your tutorials.

Because taxation law is rules-based, it is not possible to include even the majority of section and division numbers, case law and ATO Rulings that you must know and apply in lecture materials. The purpose of lectures is not to share all the course materials with you. Your primary source of the course materials is PoTL as explained in the Topic Learning guides.

Tutorials

The Tutorial Program provides you with guided opportunities to practice your skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings in-class, taking a small-group interactive approach that facilitates and encourages student participation. It is not possible to attend another tutorial group as summative tutorial assessment is linked to the tutorial group each student is enrolled in according to their personal timetable. It is your responsibility to proactively preference for a tutorial group via My Timetable (available through my.UQ dashboard) so that you choose a tutorial group that fits in with your personal circumstance.

Attendance at tutorials in Teaching Weeks 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 12 and 13 is compulsory and a pre-requisite to earn assessment marks. Students must attend the tutorial they are signed on to according to their own personal timetable. It is not possible to attend another tutorial group.

Tutorials are not recorded as students are expected to prepare for and attend their timetabled scheduled tutorial classes. Some written feedback about tutorial exercises are released.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

85% +
late_submission:
Penalties for Late Submission of Tutorials conducted in Teaching Weeks 3, 5 and 6

All students enrolled in a particular tutorial group will commence and complete their online quiz at the same time when instructed to do so by their tutor. It is not possible to complete the online quiz at a different time unless prior approval has been granted through a formal application for extension. Each quiz closes automatically at the end of the test duration. Therefore, late submission is not possible and penalties for late submission do not apply.

Penalties for Late Submission of Tutorials conducted in Teaching Weeks 4, 7, 9, 12 and 13

The following penalties apply to late submission, assuming that an extension has not been approved, and that late submission is academically possible (e.g., it does not delay feedback to a cohort, or interfere with course delivery).

Late period recorded from time submission is due

Penalty

First 7 periods of 24 hours (or part thereof)

10% per 24 hours of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item.

For example, a report worth a maximum of 40 marks, submitted 28 hours late will attract a penalty of 8 marks, calculated as 2 periods of 24 hours x 10% x 40 marks

More than 7 periods of 24 hours

100%

Extension to Assessment Due Date

An extension for an assessment item due within the teaching period in which the course is offered will generally be limited to one week in the first instance. In exceptional circumstances, approved extensions may be granted for more than one week but will not exceed four weeks in total. Where a student is incapacitated for a period exceeding four weeks of the teaching period, they should be advised to apply for removal of course.

A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances. Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply . Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .

Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.

Applications to be submitted before the due date : Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment . Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date and time, must include evidence of the reason for the late request, and will require the decision maker listed in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedure to accept the request for consideration.

Late Submission of In-Semester Digital Exam

Penalties for late submission will apply to the total mark available for an examination unless a student can demonstrate that system and/or process issues beyond their control resulted in the late submission of the examination.

Additional information on late penalties for digital examinations can be found within the Examinations Procedure .

Technical difficulties during digital assessments and technical difficulties in submitting digital assessments

It is each student's own responsibility to collect and retain evidence of technical difficulties they experience.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.
